title: This is absolutely CRAZY
summary: Matthew Berman posted a video titled "This is absolutely CRAZY" without disclosed subject matter in the provided text. The post includes links to his newsletter and social channels. The actual topic and stance remain unknown from the supplied material.
writeup: Andrej Karpathy's announcement that he is joining Anthropic prompted this analysis of what top-talent choices reveal about the AI lab landscape in 2025. The video argues that Karpathy's move, coming from an OpenAI co-founder and independent educator who had no financial incentive, functions as a public endorsement of Anthropic's pessimistic worldview: that AI is genuinely dangerous, job displacement will be severe, and only a safety-first culture can be trusted with frontier models. The through-line is a broader concern that AI pessimism, amplified by social-media engagement mechanics and political polarization, is capturing the public narrative in ways that may trigger restrictive policy precisely when US competitiveness depends on pushing forward.
key_points: Karpathy's Anthropic announcement drew 24 million views on a job-update post, a scale the video reads as evidence that the major AI labs have acquired near-religious cultural gravity, with talent choices functioning as doctrinal flags rather than career moves.

Anthropic's worldview, as characterized here, includes Dario Amodei's 'white collar bloodbath' warnings, a stated belief that AI may become sentient in a dangerous sense, opposition to open-source release, and a claim to being the only sufficiently responsible steward of frontier models; not a single Anthropic founder has left since the company was founded.

The video maps three lab ideologies: Anthropic (pessimistic, safety-paramount), OpenAI (AI as a tool enabling abundance with a painful but manageable transition), and xAI (explicitly optimistic, with Musk citing universal high income as the destination), and frames Karpathy's choice as a rejection of both OpenAI's culture and its optimistic worldview.

A Pew Research figure cited in the video shows 50% of Americans are more concerned than excited about AI, a proportion that has grown since ChatGPT launched in late 2022, and the video ties this to social-media algorithms that maximize engagement through fear and anger rather than accurate sentiment.

The video raises a geopolitical argument: fear-driven domestic policy that slows or pauses AI development cedes ground to countries that will not slow down, making public pessimism a strategic risk, not just a perception problem.

Eric Schmidt being booed at a commencement speech while making the case for AI's importance is cited as an example of how industry figures are actively worsening public perception, meaning the labs themselves share responsibility for the pessimism they now navigate.
